title: Mathematical Foundations for Economics Students
text:
Preface
This website is a development in progress to serve as an open-access mathematical compendium covering mathematical topics fundamental for any student of economics.
Version 0.0 of this resource is to include Trigonometry, Calculus I, Calculus II, and applications of such topics in the context of Economics.
This resource is designed for long-term expansion across additional, related subjects that will build upon the necessary, basic principles that Version 0.0 introduces.
Since this is an independent project that is following concurrently with my own studies, I’d like to ask that you please pardon the ongoing construction. All errors are my own.

title: 1  Basics of Trigonometry
text:
1.1 The Three Primary Functions
In mathematics, there are six fundamental trigonometric functions. For simplicity, we will begin with the three primary functions to establish the basic framework and then introduce their reciprocals later on. The three primary functions are as follows with their classic mnemonic for easy recall. \[
\begin{array}{ccc}
\textbf{SOH} & \textbf{CAH} & \textbf{TOA} \\[6pt]
\sin\theta = \dfrac{\text{opp}}{\text{hyp}} &
\cos\theta = \dfrac{\text{adj}}{\text{hyp}} &
\tan\theta = \dfrac{\text{opp}}{\text{adj}}
\end{array}
\]
You can think of sine, cosine, and tangent as ratios that answer a very specific question: given an angle \(\theta\), what are the proportions of the sides of any right triangle that contains that angle? It doesn’t matter how large or small the triangle is. For example, the ratio of opposite to hypotenuse is always the same number: \(\sin\theta\). These functions don’t give us side lengths, but rather, a ratio that is fixed to that angle. Essentially, sine, cosine, and tangent are a way to encode the shape of a triangle using a single number, the angle. The pythagorean theorem, \(a^2 + b^2 = c^2\), finds missing sides of a right triangle, with \(a\) being the side opposite from the angle, \(b\) being the side next to the angle, and \(c\) being the hypotenuse, or the longest side. Because of this connection, we can similarly write the three primary functions as: \[
\sin\theta = \frac{a}{c} \qquad
\cos\theta = \frac{b}{c} \qquad
\tan\theta = \frac{a}{b}
\]

title: 1  Basics of Trigonometry
1  Basics of Trigonometry
text:
1.3 Special Right Triangles
Special right triangles are specific triangles with fixed angles, making their side lengths intuitive without even having to use the pythagorean theorem. There are two special right triangles: the 30-60-90 and the 45-45-90. These are special because they are the only angles where the ratios come out as clean, exact numbers. Its helpful to think of them as geometric proofs of the ratios and not just a memory device. Because of their simplicity, they make it easier to analyze the unit circle and evaluate trigonometric functions.

1.3.1 Special Right Triangle #1: 30-60-90
The first special right triangle has a base length of 1, a height of \(\sqrt{3}\), and a hypotenuse of 2. Its smallest angle measures \(\frac{\pi}{6}\) degrees and the remaining angle measures \(\frac{\pi}{3}\) degrees. Below are the evaluations of sine, cosine, and tangent for the given angles:


\[
\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) = \dfrac{\text{opp}}{\text{hyp}} = \frac{1}{2} \qquad
\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right) = \dfrac{\text{opp}}{\text{hyp}} = \frac{\sqrt3}{2}
\] \[
\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) = \dfrac{\text{adj}}{\text{hyp}} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \qquad
\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right) = \dfrac{\text{adj}}{\text{hyp}} = \frac{1}{2}
\] \[
\tan\!\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) = \dfrac{\text{opp}}{\text{adj}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt3} \qquad
\tan\!\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right) = \dfrac{\text{opp}}{\text{adj}} = \frac{\sqrt3}{1}
\]
Think of these evaluations as a way of “looking up” the ratio that corresponds to the angle. It doesn’t matter if the hypotenuse is 1 centimeter or 1 kilometer, in any right triangle where one angle is \(\frac{\pi}{3}\), for example, the fraction of the hypotenuse that is the adjacent side will always be exactly half. There is no arithmetic being done or formula we are plugging values into. These evaluations read a geometric fact because these ratios will hold for the specified angle no matter what.

title: 1  Basics of Trigonometry
text:
1.4 The Unit Circle
The unit circle is a circle of radius \(r = 1\) centered at the origin of the Cartesian plane. It is the foundation of trigonometry and, by extension, of much of calculus.
For any angle \(\theta\) measured counterclockwise from the positive \(x\)-axis, the corresponding point on the unit circle is:
\[
(\cos\theta,\ \sin\theta)
\]

title: 4  Introduction to Derivatives
text:
4.1 Derivatives
A derivative measures the instantaneous rate of change of a function. At any given point, the derivative is the slope of the line tangent to that point.
